question_text: A dashboard uses a pure black (#000000) background with white text. The designer says it looks clean. Is this the right dark mode aesthetic for a premium technical dashboard?
rationale: Pure black backgrounds look dead and harsh — premium dark UIs use hue-tinted darks (purple-blacks: #0f0d1a, #13111c). Pure black also creates harsh contrast with white that causes eye strain in low-light. Not a critical failure but a clear visual design anti-pattern.

question_text: A React dashboard uses 6 different font families: Inter for body, Montserrat for headings, Roboto for labels, Courier New for code, Georgia for callouts, and Arial for buttons. Is this a design problem?
rationale: 6 font families is visual chaos. Rule: one display font + one mono font per application. Approved display fonts: Space Grotesk, Outfit, Geist Sans. Approved mono: JetBrains Mono, Fira Code. Inter, Roboto, Arial, Georgia are explicitly in the anti-patterns list.

question_text: A mobile app places its primary call-to-action button in the top-right corner of the screen. Is this good UX for a mobile interface?
rationale: Top-right is in the 'stretch zone' — hardest to reach on mobile, especially for right-handed users with large phones. Primary CTAs belong in the bottom 1/3 of the screen (easy thumb reach). Top-right is appropriate only for infrequent actions like settings or profile.

question_text: A KPI dashboard card shows a large number (54,081 page views) with an inline sparkline and a trend badge (+8.2% vs last week). The card uses glass surface styling with subtle border. Is this the right component pattern?
rationale: This is the canonical KPI card pattern: large number (thin weight), inline sparkline (80x32), trend badge with TrendingUp icon, glass surface. Correct implementation of the stat card pattern.

question_text: A grid layout has 6 identical cards, each the same size with the same amount of content. The designer calls this a 'bento grid'. Is this correct?
rationale: Identical cards in a uniform grid is NOT a bento grid — it's just a grid. Bento grids use mixed col-span and row-span to create asymmetry: one dominant chart (2x2), smaller stat cards (1x1), a wide table (2x1). Asymmetry is the defining characteristic. This is an AI-generated anti-pattern.

question_text: A form uses `outline: none` on all focused input elements for aesthetic reasons. Is this acceptable?
rationale: Removing focus outlines is hostile to keyboard users and fails WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible). Keyboard and switch-device users rely on focus indicators for navigation. Fix: use :focus-visible instead of :focus to show rings only for keyboard navigation, not mouse clicks.

question_text: A web app uses shadcn/ui components with their default styling throughout the interface. Is this acceptable for a premium dark dashboard?
rationale: The design philosophy explicitly prohibits component library defaults — DaisyUI, shadcn, and any library that produces template-looking interfaces are forbidden. Raw Tailwind with custom cva-based components is required. shadcn default styling looks like a template, not a premium product.

question_text: A button component has a hover transition duration of 400ms. Is this appropriate for a button interaction?
rationale: 400ms hover transition makes the UI feel sluggish and unresponsive. Button hover responses should be 100-150ms (instant-feeling). 400ms is appropriate for page transitions or modal opens, not micro-interactions. Slow hover transitions violate the 'animations without purpose that delay interaction' anti-pattern.

question_text: A dashboard sidebar uses icon-only navigation at 64px width with tooltips on hover showing the label. The active item has a cyan accent bar on the left edge. Is this the correct sidebar pattern?
rationale: This matches the canonical icon sidebar pattern exactly: 60-64px wide, icon-only, active indicator as accent bar on left edge, tooltips on hover. Logo top, settings bottom, with accent tint on the active icon.

question_text: A data table component shows a spinner overlay the entire page while loading data for just one table on a multi-section dashboard. Is this good UX?
rationale: Full-page spinner for a single component blocks the entire interface unnecessarily. Loading states should be scoped to the component loading: skeleton rows inside the table, rest of the dashboard remains interactive. Full-page spinners are an anti-pattern except during initial page load.

question_text: A touch target for a delete button in a mobile list item is 24x24px. The adjacent edit button is also 24x24px with 4px gap between them. Is this acceptable for mobile?
rationale: Minimum touch target is 44x44px (iOS) / 48x48dp (Android). 24px targets cause frequent mis-taps. The 4px gap between adjacent targets compounds this. Both targets need to be at least 44px with at minimum 8px gap between them.

question_text: An empty state in a data table shows 'No records found.' in small gray text. Is this a complete empty state implementation?
rationale: Empty states must never just say 'No data found.' A complete empty state requires: large muted icon/illustration that communicates the context, helpful explanatory text (why is it empty, what can the user do), and a CTA button (e.g., 'Add your first record'). Text-only empty states are a design anti-pattern.

question_text: A chart component uses `linear` easing on its animated entrance. Is this appropriate?
rationale: 'Never linear for UI motion' is in the explicit anti-patterns list. Linear easing feels mechanical and robotic. For chart entrance, use ease-out (natural deceleration) or a spring animation. The chart still renders correctly — this is an aesthetic/polish issue, not a critical UX failure.

question_text: A design uses cyan #38bdf8 as the primary accent on dark #0f0d1a backgrounds. Typography uses Space Grotesk for display and JetBrains Mono for code values. Is this a well-formed design system?
rationale: Cyan #38bdf8 on #0f0d1a is the canonical primary accent from the design system. Space Grotesk (display) + JetBrains Mono (code) is the approved font pairing. This matches the retro sunset palette and design system standards exactly.

question_text: A component library uses inline styles (style={{ color: '#38bdf8', padding: '16px' }}) throughout instead of Tailwind classes. Is this acceptable?
rationale: Inline styles are explicitly forbidden — use Tailwind classes only. Inline styles cannot leverage design tokens, cannot be overridden with className, create specificity conflicts, and are inconsistent with the dark mode (dark:) system. Every color and spacing value must go through Tailwind utility classes.
